source_quote: Board completely unresponsive after being left plugged-in while off — no LCD, no LPC wakeup indication, nothing happens when the power key is pressed. Likely cause: LPC system controller enters an unrecoverable state; a firmware bug prevents it from leaving its locked-up condition. Try the LPC reset button (nearest the battery connector); if that fails, the only reliable recovery is disconnecting and reconnecting the battery cables.

evidence.source_quote_substring: LPC system controller enters an unrecoverable state
evidence.reasoning: BT1 is the battery/RTC component; the LPC controller is described as entering an unrecoverable state. Using BT1 as the nearest registry refdes for the system controller node. The community maps LPC to BT1 in the graph context.

evidence.source_quote_substring: nothing happens when the power key is pressed
evidence.reasoning: The LPC controller is completely non-responsive — a dead state where it cannot process inputs or issue wakeup signals.

source_quote: It stopped working: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark. Verify cell voltages individually; reflash LPC firmware via a second computer.

evidence.reasoning: D9 is explicitly named in the research as the power indicator LED and exists as D9 in the graph. The symptom is D9 being dark (dead/not lit).

evidence.source_quote_substring: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark
evidence.reasoning: D9 LED not illuminating despite valid supply connected — dead mode for the indicator, indicating either the LED itself or its drive path has failed.

source_quote: One battery cell may be leaking slightly, leaving a tiny dark spot visible on the cover. Visual inspection of cells through the acrylic bottom is sufficient to identify the affected cell.

evidence.source_quote_substring: One battery cell may be leaking slightly
evidence.reasoning: J1 is the connector linking the battery cells to the board. A leaking cell degrades the effective source impedance seen at J1, modelled as a leaky short.

evidence.source_quote_substring: leaving a tiny dark spot visible on the cover
evidence.reasoning: A leaking cell implies internal degradation causing a resistive leakage path — matching leaky_short mode.

evidence.source_quote_substring: One battery cell may be leaking slightly
evidence.reasoning: Slight leakage consistent with a high-resistance internal path; modelled at 200 Ω typical for a degraded cell.

source_quote: Attaching a camera, mouse, or NFC reader produces nothing — no power, no data. Try all ports; a partially functional port (enumeration errors only) can help isolate whether the fault is in the VBUS switch or the data path.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the fault is in the VBUS switch or the data path
evidence.reasoning: U24 is the source of the USB_PWR rail in the graph (kind=ic, decoupling=C69,C76,C71,C77). The VBUS switch for USB power is the most likely failure point when USB ports show no power or data.
Complete absence of power and data on USB ports — U24 (the USB power switch/hub) is dead, cutting the USB_PWR rail.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Attaching a camera, mouse, or NFC reader produces nothing — no power, no data
evidence.reasoning: USB_PWR rail from U24 would be dead, leaving attached devices without VBUS.

source_quote: MNT Reform Motherboard Repair Logs — common failure clusters centre on the LPC system controller, battery management circuitry, display interconnects, and USB power paths.

evidence.source_quote_substring: battery management circuitry, display interconnects, and USB power paths
evidence.reasoning: U20 is the source of PCIE1_PWR in the graph. PCIe power paths are part of the board's power distribution and can fail similarly to USB power paths mentioned in the repair logs.

evidence.source_quote_substring: common failure clusters centre on the LPC system controller, battery management circuitry
evidence.reasoning: Power management ICs like U20 can fail dead, cutting the PCIE1_PWR rail to the mini PCIe or M.2 slot.

evidence.source_quote_substring: battery management circuitry, display interconnects, and USB power paths
evidence.reasoning: PCIE1_PWR would be lost if U20 fails dead.
